slides.title: Computing AlgorithmsLecture 4:Brute force part 1
Agenda
content: Sequential search and brute-force string matching

slides.title: What is a Brute force strategy
content: Brute force  is a straightforward approach to solving a problem, usually directly based on the problem statement and definitions of the concepts involved.
The algorithm tries  all possible solutions  until it finds the correct one.
It is based directly on the problem’s definition, without using optimization, heuristics, or shortcuts

slides.title: Key Characteristics of Brute-Force Algorithms
content: Simple to design — they follow directly from the problem statement.
Guaranteed correctness — if a solution exists, brute force will find it.
Often inefficient — can be slow for large input sizes because the number of possibilities grows quickly.

slides.title: Examples
content: Password cracking: Trying every possible combination until the correct password is found.
Searching: Linear search checks each element of a list until the target is found.
Combinatorial problems: Generating all permutations or subsets to find an optimal one (e.g., traveling salesman problem).

slides.title: When Brute Force Is Useful
content: When the input size is small.
When no faster algorithm is known.
As a simple baseline to compare more advanced algorithms.

slides.title: Selection sort
content: Let’s trace the selection sort on this given array  of size n=5
First the for loop  i =0  till it reach n-2 or in this case 3

slides.title: Selection sort
content: min is assigned the value of  i =0

slides.title: Selection sort
content: Starting the inner loop where j will start from j=i+1 or j=0+1=1 till it reaches n-1 or 4

slides.title: Selection sort
content: Check the condition for A[j] < A[min] or A[1]< A[0] = 21<14 which is false

slides.title: Selection sort
content: We go back to the inner loop and increment the variable j by 1 where j =2, which still didn’t reach more than n-1 or 4

slides.title: Selection sort
content: Check the condition for A[j] < A[min] or A[2]< A[0] = 1<14, which is true, then we change the min value to 2

slides.title: Selection sort
content: We go back to the inner loop and increment the variable j by 1, where j =3, which still didn’t reach more than n-1 or 4

slides.title: Selection sort
content: Check the condition for A[j] < A[min] or A[3]< A[2] = 12<1 which is false

slides.title: Selection sort
content: We go back to the inner loop and increment the variable j by 1, where j =4, which still didn’t reach more than n-1 or 4

slides.title: Selection sort
content: Check the condition for A[j] < A[min] or A[4]< A[2] = 14<1 which is false

slides.title: Selection sort
content: We go back to the inner loop and increment the variable j by 1, where j =5, which reached more than n-1 or 4

slides.title: Selection sort
content: The innermost loop is finished now we move to swap A[ i ] and A[min] which will swap A[0] and A[2] or 14 with 1

slides.title: Selection sort
content: The outer loop increases  i  by 1, it will be equal to 1, and still didn’t reach more than n-2 or 3

slides.title: Selection sort
content: min is assigned the value of  i =1

slides.title: Selection sort
content: The inner loop index j will be reset to i+1 which will be equal to j=2 till it reaches more than n-1 or 4

slides.title: Selection sort
content: Check the condition for A[j] < A[min] or A[2]< A[1] = 14<21, which is true, so the new value of min is 2

slides.title: Selection sort
content: The outer loop increases  i  by 1, it will be equal to 1, and it still didn’t reach more than n-2 or 3

slides.title: Selection sort
content: Check the condition for A[j] < A[min] or A[3]< A[2] = 12<14, which is true, so the new value of min is 3

slides.title: Selection sort
content: We go back to the inner loop and increment the variable j by 1, where j =4, which didn't reach more than n-1 or 4

slides.title: Selection sort
content: Check the condition for A[j] < A[min] or A[4]< A[3] = 14<12 which is false.

slides.title: Selection sort
content: We go back to the inner loop and increment the variable j by 1, where j =5, which reaches more than n-1 or 4

slides.title: Selection sort
content: The innermost loop is finished now we move to swap A[ i ] and A[min] which will swap A[1] and A[3] or 21 with 12

slides.title: Selection sort
content: The outer loop increases  i  by 1, it will be equal to 2, and it still didn’t reach more than n-2 or 3

slides.title: Selection sort
content: Reset the value of min is equal to  i =2

slides.title: Selection sort
content: We go back to the inner loop and reset the value of j to i+1, equal to 3 till it reaches more than n-1 or 4

slides.title: Selection sort
content: Check the condition for A[j] < A[min] or A[3]< A[2] = 21<14 which is false.

slides.title: Selection sort
content: We go back to the inner loop and increment the variable j by 1, where j =4, which still didn’t reach more than n-1 or 4

slides.title: Selection sort
content: Check the condition for A[j] < A[min] or A[4]< A[2] = 14<14 which is false.

slides.title: Selection sort
content: We go back to the inner loop and increment the variable j by 1, where j =5, which  reached more than n-1 or 4

slides.title: Selection sort
content: The innermost loop is finished now we move to swap A[ i ] and A[min] which will swap A[2] and A[2] where no change is required

slides.title: Selection sort
content: We go back to the outermost loop, where we reach the final iteration, where  i =3 till it reach more than n-2 or 3

slides.title: Selection sort
content: The min value is reset to be equal to  i  or 3

slides.title: Selection sort
content: The value for j is reset to be equal to i+1 or 4 till it reaches more than n-1 or 4

slides.title: Selection sort
content: Check the condition for A[j] < A[min] or A[4]< A[3] = 21<14, which is true, so the new value of min is 4.

slides.title: Selection sort
content: The value of j is increased by 1 to be equal to 5 which is more than n-1 or 4

slides.title: Selection sort
content: The innermost loop is finished now we move to swap A[ i ] and A[min] which will swap A[3] and A[4] or 21 with 14.

slides.title: Selection sort
content: the value of i is increased by 1, equal to 4, which is more than n-2 or 3, and the outermost loop stops

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Now, let’s trace the bubble sort on the same array
Starting from outermost loop where  i =0 till  it reach more than n-2 or 3

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Next, we move to inner loop where j=0 till it reach n-2-i or n-2-0= n-2 or 3.
This algorithm differs from the selection sort as it swaps two adjacent numbers when the lower index is greater in value.

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Check if A[j+1]<A[j] which is equal  A[1]<A[0] or 21<14 which is false

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Increase the value of j by 1 then j =1.till it reaches a value greater than n-2-0 or 5-2-0=3

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Check if A[j+1]<A[j] which is equal  A[2]<A[1] or 1<21 which is true then we swap A[2] with A[1] or 21 and 1

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Increase the value of j by 1 then j =2.till it reaches a value greater than n-2-0 or 5-2-0=3

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Check if A[j+1]<A[j] which is equal  A[3]<A[2] or 1<21 which is true then we swap A[3] with A[2] or 21 and 12

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Increase the value of j by 1 then j =3.till it reaches a value greater than n-2-0 or 5-2-0=3

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Check if A[j+1]<A[j] which is equal  A[4]<A[3] or 1<21 which is true then we swap A[4] with A[3] or 14 and 21

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Increase the value of j by 1 then j =4.which is more than n-2-0 or 5-2-0=3.

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: We go back to the outer loop and increase the value of  i  by 1, which will be equal to 1 till it reaches more than n-2 or 3.

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: In the inner loop, j will start from 0 till it till it reaches a value greater than n-2-i or 5-2-1=2

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Check if A[j+1]<A[j] which is equal  A[1]<A[0] or 1<14 which is true then we swap A[1] with A[0] or 14 and 1

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Increase the value of j by 1 then j =1.which is less than n-2-i or 5-2-1=2.

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Check if A[j+1]<A[j] which is equal  A[2]<A[1] or 12<14 which is true then we swap A[2] with A[1] or 12 and 14

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Increase the value of j by 1 then j =2.which is equal to n-2-0 or 5-2-1=2.

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Check if A[j+1]<A[j] which is equal  A[3]<A[2] or 14<14 which is false, so no swap is going to be performed

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Increase the value of j by 1 then j =3.which is more than n-2-i or 5-2-1=2.

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: We go back to the outer loop and increase the value of  i  by 1, which will be equal to 2 till it is greater than n-2 or 3.

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: In the inner loop, j will start from 0 till it till it reaches a value greater than n-2-i or 5-2-2=1

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Check if A[j+1]<A[j], which is equal  A[1]<A[0] or 12<1 which is false, so no swap is going to be performed

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Increase the value of j by 1 then j =1.which is equal to n-2-i or 5-2-2=1.

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Check if A[j+1]<A[j], which is equal  A[2]<A[1] or 14<12 which is false, so no swap is going to be performed

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Increase the value of j by 1 then j =2.which is more than n-2-i or 5-2-2=1.

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: We go back to the outer loop and increase the value of  i  by 1, which will be equal to 3 till it is greater than n-2 or 3.

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: In the inner loop, j will start from 0 till it till it reaches a value greater than or equal to n-2-i or 5-2-3=0

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: Check if A[j+1]<A[j], which is equal  A[1]<A[0] or 12<1 which is false, so no swap needed

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: The value j is increased by 1, where j=1, which is more than n-2-i or 5-2-3=0.

slides.title: Bubble sort
content: We go back to the outer loop and increase the value of  i  by 1, which will be equal to 4 till it reaches more than n-2 or 3.

slides.title: Sequential Search
content: Given that the key we are looking for is equal to 6 in the given array
The first step is to append the key to the end of the list

slides.title: Sequential Search
content: Create an index  i  which starts from 0

slides.title: Sequential Search
content: Check if the value of A[ i ]=A[0]=1 is not equal to k; which is true

slides.title: Sequential Search
content: Increment the value of  i  by 1, it will be equal 1

slides.title: Sequential Search
content: Check if the value of A[i]=A[1]=16 is not equal to k=6; which is true

slides.title: Sequential Search
content: Increment the value of  i  by 1, it will be equal 2

slides.title: Sequential Search
content: Check if the value of A[i]=A[2]=6 is not equal to k=6; which is false, and that we found the key.

slides.title: Sequential Search
content: Then we move to the if condition that checks if  i  is less than n. Since this is true, it returns the index  i =2 where the key 6 is found.

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Where n is the length of Text equal 6
m is the length of the pattern, equal 2
The outer loop starts from  i =0 till n-m which is 6-2=4

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Check the condition for the while loop first, j<m  0 < 2. which is true for the second part P[ i ]=T[ i+j ] p[0]=T[0+0] or ‘b’=‘e’ which is false.

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Check if condition j=m or 0=2 which is false
We go to outer for loop

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Increment the value of  i  by 1 till n-m, which is 6-2=4

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Check the condition for the while loop first, j<m  0 < 2. which is true for the second part P[ i ]=T[ i+j ] p[0]=T[i+0] or ‘e’=‘e’ which is true.

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Check the condition for the while loop first, j<m  0 < 2. which is true for the second part P[ i ]=T[ i+j ] p[0]=T[1+1] or ‘t’=‘r’ which is false.

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Check if condition j=m or 1=2 which is false
We go to the outer for-loop

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Increment the value of  i  by 1 till n-m, which is 6-2=4

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Check the condition for the while loop first, j<m  0 < 2. which is true for the second part P[ i ]=T[ i+j ] p[0]=T[2+0] or ‘t’=‘e’ which is false.

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Check if condition j=m or 0=2 which is false
We go to outer for loop

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Increment the value of  i  by 1 till n-m, which is 6-2=4

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Check the condition for the while loop first, j<m  0 < 2. which is true for the second part P[ i ]=T[ i+j ] p[0]=T[3+0] or ‘t’=‘e’ which is false.

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Check if condition j=m or 0=2 which is false
We go to outer for loop

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Increment the value of  i  by 1 till n-m, which is 6-2=4

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Check the condition for the while loop first, j<m  0 < 2. which is true for the second part P[ i ]=T[ i+j ] p[0]=T[4+0] or ‘e’=‘e’ which is true.

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Check the condition for the while loop first, j<m  1 < 2. which is true for the second part P[ i ]=T[ i+j ] p[0]=T[4+1] or ‘r’=‘r’ which is true.

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Check the condition for the while loop first, j<m  2 < 2. which is false and we exit the loop

slides.title: Brute Force String Match
content: Check if condition j=m or 2=2 which is  ture
So we return the index  i  = 2

slides.title: Brute Force String Match (Time analysis)
content: Standard mathematical analysis for this algorithm can be considered  impractical  because the actual number of basic operations depends entirely on the specific content of the  two input parameters  (the text and the pattern).

So we can take the probability of matching one character in the English language is 1/26, which is actually very low, so the average case can be approximated as θ(n) or more accurately θ( n+m ). n is the number of mismatches, and m is the number of matches of the pattern with the text.
The worst case, which is a rare occurrence, for example, a pattern of ‘ aaab ’ and text of all a’s (‘ aaaaaaa …….’). In this case, it will be almost O( n.m )
